coredata.dc:title: Immobilization of papain on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots improves the enzymatic properties and makes it a biosensor for cystatin C
coredata.dc:creator: Mirzaei, Farzaneh
Valizadeh, Hassan
Pazhang, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In recent years enzymes had many applications, such as catalysts in organic synthesis and producing biosensors. In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were functionalized with glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu) and used as a support for the immobilization of papain (NGQD-Glu-Papain). Immobilization of papain on NGQDs has considerably improved papain stability. So, with incubation of the enzymes at 70 °C, immobilized papain maintained 80% of its activity for 150 min while the free enzyme's residual activity was 55% after 50 min. The stability results in the presence of organic solvents showed that the immobilization increased papain stability, especially in polar solvents. The activity results showed that the Km value decreases from 2.02 g L−1 to 1.20 g L−1 after immobilization, and the Vmax value decreases from 932.1 μmol mg−1 min−1 to 62.5 μmol mg−1 min−1. The fluorimetric studies showed that the NGQD-Glu-Papain is specific sensitive to cystatin C at concentrations ranging from 0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1 with a 0.05 ng L−1 of limit for detection. In conclusion, NGQDs can be suitable for enzyme stabilization and biosensor production.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The properties of the prepared nanoparticles. Analysis of the crystalline structure with X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), NGQD-activated glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu), and the immobilized papain on NGQD-Glu (NGQD-Glu-Papain) (A). The study of the morphology of NGQDs with transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (B) and field emission scanning electron microscopes (FE-SEM) (C), and FE-SEM for the NGQD-Glu-Papain (D).

The activity of free and immobilized papain. The activity of the free and immobilized enzymes in the different pHs (A) and at different temperatures (B). Michaelis-Menten plots of the free Papain (C) and immobilized Papain (D), the insert is the Lineweaver-Burk plot. (Standard deviations were 3% lower than experimental values).

The stability of the enzymes. The enzymes stability in different temperatures (A), and the time-course stability of the enzyme at 70 °C (B). The storage stability of free (C) and immobilized papain(D). The free (E) and immobilized papain (F) stability in the presence of the organic solvent. Reusability of immobilized papain (G). (Standard deviations were 3% lower than experimental values).

The fluorimetric properties of the prepared nanoparticles. The comparison of fluorescence intensity of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs), NGQD-activated glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu), and the immobilized papain on NGQD-Glu (NGQD-Glu-Papain) (A). fluorescence emission spectra of NGQD-Glu-Papain to detect cystatin C with different concentrations of cystatin C (0.05 ng L−1, 0.5 ng L−1, 5 ng L−1, 50 ng L−1) (B). The relationship between the fluorescence intensity ratio (F-F0)/F0 and the logarithmic value of cystatin C concentration C (C). The fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain biosensor in the presence of two concentrations of cystatin C and other proteins have been measured to study of biosensor specificity (D).
Kinetic parameters of free and immobilized papain.
The comparison of the immobilized papain on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dot-activated glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu-Papain) biosensor with the other biosensors to detect cystatin C (papain base).

head.ce:title: Immobilization of papain on nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots improves the enzymatic properties and makes it a biosensor for cystatin C

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years enzymes had many applications, such as catalysts in organic synthesis and producing biosensors. In this study, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) were functionalized with glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu) and used as a support for the immobilization of papain (NGQD-Glu-Papain). Immobilization of papain on NGQDs has considerably improved papain stability. So, with incubation of the enzymes at 70 °C, immobilized papain maintained 80% of its activity for 150 min while the free enzyme's residual activity was 55% after 50 min. The stability results in the presence of organic solvents showed that the immobilization increased papain stability, especially in polar solvents. The activity results showed that the Km value decreases from 2.02 g L−1 to 1.20 g L−1 after immobilization, and the Vmax value decreases from 932.1 μmol mg−1 min−1 to 62.5 μmol mg−1 min−1. The fluorimetric studies showed that the NGQD-Glu-Papain is specific sensitive to cystatin C at concentrations ranging from 0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1 with a 0.05 ng L−1 of limit for detection. In conclusion, NGQDs can be suitable for enzyme stabilization and biosensor production.

ce:section.ce:para: The unique structural properties of graphene quantum dots (GQDs), such as high availability surface area, chemical structure, fluorescence properties, good water solubility, high thermal stability, and low cytotoxicity, have attracted intense research interest [1]. The GQDs have been used to support for the immobilization of enzymes or proteins in rare cases such as glucose oxidase and hemoglobin [2,3]. Doping of heterogeneous atoms in GQDs is a successful approach to enhancing and controlling the optical and electrical properties [4]. Nitrogen has attracted interest in doping graphene quantum dots to improve fluorescence performance while maintaining graphene properties [5]. GQDs, and nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (NGQDs) have many applications, such as selective fluorescence sensors, biomedical applications, and other industrial applications [6,7].

In recent years, enzymes have been used in many applications such as biosensors, efficient biological catalysts in green chemical synthesis, food processing, pharmaceuticals, medicine, and other industrial fields [8–10]. However, disadvantages such as high cost, inability to recover, temperature sensitivity, and low activity and stability in organic solvents limit the industrial application of the enzymes [11,12]. Enzyme immobilization is a valuable technique for solving these problems, increasing industrial applications, and improving enzymatic properties [13,14] . Various immobilization strategies are used for immobilization of enzymes [15] such as encapsulation [16], adsorption [17], covalent [18], and cross-linking [19]. After immobilization, these methods have shown variable influences on enzyme properties. Therefore, selecting the immobilization method is a crucial approach for effective immobilization. The enzyme’s immobilization on the supports without a cross-linker leads to low covalent absorption. Therefore, using a cross-linker such as glutaraldehyde for covalent immobilization can increase the loaded enzyme on the supports [20,21]. Prominent properties of the supports are low cost, stability, high binding capacity, and, most importantly, maintenance of biological activity of the immobilized enzyme [22]. Today, various supports have been used to immobilize enzymes, such as magnetic nanomaterials, carbon nanotubes, graphene oxide, organic-inorganic nanocomposite, and many other supports [23,24]. However, based on the physical and chemical properties of the supports, there are some limitations in the immobilization of enzymes, such as lower binding of the enzyme to supports (low loading) and lower relative activity of immobilized enzymes compared to the free enzyme [25]. Graphene quantum dots can be selected as appropriate support for immobilization and adsorption of the enzyme due to their unique properties such as large surface area, available functional groups, easy chemical modification capability, and electron-donating properties [2,26,27].

Papain is a natural plant cysteine protease extracted from Carica papaya and is one of biotechnology's most critical industrial enzymes [28]. It has attracted much attention due to its excellent enzymatic activity, antioxidant activity, high-temperature stability, safety, and non-toxicity [28,29]. Papain is applied in the chemical, leather, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic industries [30,31]. So far, papain has been immobilized on many organic and inorganic supports such as agarose and cellulose beads, chitosan, polymers, silica, and many other supports [32]. The immobilization of papain increases the enzyme stability and makes it recoverable from the reaction media [28,30].

Nowadays, sensors have numerous applications in industry and medicine. Recently, papain was used to produce a biosensor for measuring cystatin C [30]. Cystatin C is one of the most critical extracellular proteins to inhibit cysteine proteases. The concentration of cystatin C in human urine and blood is from 0.03 μg mL−1 to 0.29 μg mL−1 and 0.57 μg mL−1 to 1.79 μg mL−1, respectively. Changes in the concentration of cystatin C in urine and blood indicate some diseases [33]. Cystatin C can only be removed from the bloodstream by glomerular filtration in the kidneys of the human body. Studies have shown that serum cystatin C levels are independent of individual conditions such as age and gender. Therefore, serum cystatin C levels can be used as an accurate diagnostic test for glomerular filtration rate (GFR) to assess renal function in chronic kidney disease (CKD). As a result, the levels of cystatin C can be used to monitor kidney disease [30]. Parkinson's disease (PD) has recently been diagnosed by assessing serum cystatin C [34,35]. Cystatin C has a high specific affinity to papain [30] and inhibits papain's activity when the N-terminus of cystatin C is attached to the enzyme catalytic center [36].

In this study, the covalent immobilization of papain was performed on NGQDs using glutaraldehyde as a linker to produce NGQD-Glu-Papain nanoparticles. Then the enzymatic activity and stability of the immobilized papain were examined to investigate its catalyst application. Finally, the fluorimetric properties of NGQD-Glu-Papain were studied to investigate its ability to sense cystatin C.

The Citric acid monohydrate (ACS, ISO, Reag. Ph Eur), urea (ACS), glycine, succinic acid (ChP, NF, JPE, ACS), imidazole, tris-(hydroxymethyl)-aminomethane (Ph Eur, BP, ChP, JPC, USP), bovine serum albumin (Diagnostic Grade), cystatin C, and human serum albumin (HSA) were obtained from the Merck Company (Germany). Papain (powder, ≥ 30,000 U mg−1), glutaraldehyde (25% v v−1), casein, and trichloroacetic acid (ACS reagent, ≥ 99.0%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). The enzymatic solutions were prepared using 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5).

The nanoparticle morphological studies were performed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) (Hitachi, model S-4160) and transmission electron microscope (Zeiss-EM10C-100Kv made in Germany). Optical properties were measured by an FP-6200 Spectrofluorometer (JASCO Corporation, Japan). Fourier transforms infrared (FT-IR) spectra with KBr were recorded with an FT-IR spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer). X-ray diffraction (XRD) was conducted using XRD manufactured by Bruker Global Offices (AXS, model D8). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was carried out using TGA 209 F3Tarsus under atmosphere N2. Microwave experiments were conducted in a landmark Microsynth device.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The NGQDs were synthesized using a facility and fast bottom-up method with microwave-assisted [37]. The citric acid (CA) and urea were used as carbon and nitrogen precursors. 2 g of citric acid and 1.2 g of urea (1:2 molar ratio) were dissolved in 3 mL of deionized water. Then were placed in the microwave cell and heated under microwave irradiation to 180 °C with full power (1000 W) for 120 min; after heating, they were allowed to cool to room temperature. The dark brown solution was centrifuged at 10,000g with ethanol to remove unreacted urea. Then the solution was dried in an oven at 70 °C, and the dark power was stored at room temperature.

Due to the amine groups on the surface of NGQDs, glutaraldehyde was selected as a cross-linker [38]. Then, the NGQDs surface was functionalized with glutaraldehyde to increase the covalent immobilization [32]. Briefly, in an ultrasonic bath, a glutaraldehyde solution (5 mL, 6% v v−1) was slowly added to 0.2 g of NGQDs in 5 mL of 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) to achieve the final concentration of glutaraldehyde up to 3% v v−1 in the reaction mixture. The solution was stirred for 2 h at room temperature. The results showed that glutaraldehyde with a concentration of 3% v v−1 causes the most immobilization of papain (the results not shown). With the increase in the density (concentration) of glutaraldehyde, inter-molecular binding between glutaraldehyde molecules increases and causes lower covalent enzyme immobilization [39]. Therefore, in this work, the relatively low concentration (density) of the glutaraldehyde (3% v v−1) was used for enzyme immobilization.

The NGQD-activated glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu) was then separated by centrifugation at 10,000g for 5 min. Due to prevent the decrease of NGQD-Glu performance in the enzyme immobilization, the prepared NGQD-Glu was used for immobilization quickly after preparation. In the following, the precipitant (0.2 gr of NGQD-Glu) was dispersed into 15 mL of papain solution (3 mg mL−1) in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). Then, the solution was stirred at 4 °C (to maintain the enzyme stability) for 24 h. It should be noted that the free enzyme was incubated at 4 °C for 24 h, and the result showed that the enzyme activity was not changed significantly (the result was not shown). Finally, the product resulting from the immobilization of papain on NGQD-activated glutaraldehyde was called NGQD-Glu-Papain, separated by centrifugation at 10,000g for 5 min. It was washed with a 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) to remove the free papain. The immobilized papain was stored at 4 °C until use.

The immobilization yield (based-on protein binding) was calculated as a following:
Immobilization Yield (%) = (amount of immobilized enzyme / amount of enzyme added) × 100·

The amount of loading enzyme was calculated as a following:
Loading of enzyme = (amount of enzyme-bound in mg-protein / g of support) [40]·
It should be noted that different concentrations of papain (1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 mg mL−1) were investigated, and the maximum loading of the enzyme showed in the 3 mg mL−1 concentration of papain.

The relative activity of the immobilized enzyme (relative to the free enzyme) was calculated as follows:
Relative activity = (the specificity of immobilized enzyme/the free enzyme) × 100·

The free and immobilized enzyme's proteolytic activity was determined using casein as a substrate. The enzyme reaction was carried out in a microtube containing 450 μl of casein solution (1% w v−1) in a 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). First, 50 μl of the enzyme, free (1 mg mL−1) or immobilized enzyme (with enzyme concentration of 7 mg mL−1), was added to the solution. After incubation at 25 °C for 10 min, 500 μl of trichloroacetic acid (10% w v−1) was added to stop the enzymatic activity, and the solution was centrifuged with 10,000g for 6 min. Then, the absorption of the supernatant was measured at 280 nm. The unit of the enzyme activity has been defined as the amount of enzyme required to release one µmol of tyrosine at 25 °C in one min [41].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of optimal pH and temperature and kinetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of pH on the free and immobilized papain activity was determined in the different buffers, pH (3–10), as described previously [42].

To determine the effect of temperature on the activity of free and immobilized papain, 50 μl of the enzyme solution was added to 450 μl of reaction mixture containing casein (1% w v−1) in the 50 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). The mixture was incubated at different temperatures (25–90 °C. Finally, the enzyme activity was measured as described above.

Different concentrations of casein (0.5, 1, 2.5, 5, 10, 15, and 20 g L−1) as a substrate in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) were used to determine kinetic parameters of the free and immobilized papain. The Michaelis constant (Km) and maximum enzyme velocity (Vmax) were estimated using the Michaelis-Menten and Lineweaver-Burk curves [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of immobilization on the stability of the enzymes
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the effect of temperature on the enzyme's thermal stability, the enzymes were incubated at the different temperature ranges (25–90 °C) in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) for 30 min. The solutions were allowed to cool on ice for 30 min, and then the remaining activity was determined as described above.

To measure the time-course thermal stability of the free and immobilized papain, the enzymes were incubated in the absence and presence of the solvents (10% v v−1) at 70 °C for different times (0–60 min for the free enzyme and 0–150 min for the immobilized enzyme). After incubation, the solutions cooled on ice for 30 min; then, the remaining activities were determined as described above. The enzymes activity incubated on ice was considered 100%.

To investigate the effect of organic solvents (DMSO, DMF, THF, and ethanol) on the stability of free and immobilized papain, the enzyme solution containing sodium phosphate buffer (50 mM, pH 7.5) and 10% organic solvent (v v−1) was prepared. Then, the solution was incubated at 70 °C for different time courses (0–120 min). Finally, after cooling on ice for 30 min, the residual activity of each sample was measured as described above. The activity of the free or immobilized enzymes incubated on ice was considered 100%.

The reusability of the immobilized enzyme was determined as follows. Firstly 50 μl of the immobilized enzyme (7 mg mL−1) was added into a microtube containing 450 μl of casein solution (1% w v−1) in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). After incubation at 25 °C for 10 min, the enzyme was separated by centrifugation with 10,000g for 6 min and reused for the other cycles. Then 500 μl of trichloroacetic acid (10% w v−1) was added to the supernatant to stop the enzymatic activity and again centrifuged with 10,000g for 6 min. The absorption of the supernatant was measured at 280 nm to determine the enzyme activity. The activity of the immobilized enzyme in the first cycle was considered 100%.

Fluorometric properties of NGQDs and NGQD-Glu-Papain were studied in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5) in excitation wavelengths 340–500 nm and emission range from 360 nm to 700 nm. Different concentrations of cystatin C were prepared to evaluate the effect of cystatin C on the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain. Initially, the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain was measured at a wavelength of 400 nm and then mixed with different concentrations of cystatin C (0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1). After 5 min, the fluorescence intensity was repeated at an excitation wavelength of 400 nm. Subsequently, the difference in fluorescence intensity was investigated. All fluorescence intensity measurements were recorded in the same condition [44]. After adding cystatin C, the best time for maximum fluorescence intensity recovery was 5 min. The fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain was measured in two concentrations (0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1) of different proteins, including bovine serum albumin (BSA), papain, and human serum albumin (HSA), at 25 °C, to evaluate biosensor sensitivity.

FT-IR spectrums are used to determine functional groups ( Fig. 1, supplementary material). FT-IR spectra of NGQDs showed clear absorption bands at 3423 cm−1, 3207 cm−1, 2925 cm−1, 1662 cm−1, 1566 cm−1, and 1401 cm−1, corresponding to OH, N-H, C–H, CO, CC, and C–N stretching vibrations, respectively. These bands conﬁrm the presence of the –OH, –NH2, and COOH groups on the surface of NGQDs [45].

The FT-IR spectra of papain showed an absorption band at 3382 cm−1, which corresponds to the stretching vibration of O-H and N-H in the hydroxyl and amine groups (Fig. 1, supplementary material). The peak at 1651 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of CO (amide I band), and the peak at 1544 cm−1 corresponds to the bending vibration of N-H (amide ΙΙ band) [17].

After chemical modification of the surface of NGQDs with glutaraldehyde (NGQD-Glu), the stretching vibration of the N-H bond at 3207 cm−1 on the NGQDs disappeared (Fig. 1, supplementary material). The stretching vibration of the CN bond emerges at 1641 cm−1, which confirms the successful linkage between the free NH2 group of NGQDs and the –CHO group of glutaraldehyde [40]. The peak signal was 1709 cm−1 representing the free aldehydes group [46]. The new band appeared at 1114 cm−1, and 1070 cm−1, corresponding to C-O in glutaraldehyde [47].

After immobilization of the papain on the NGQD-Glu, the protein absorption bands were seen at 1641 cm−1 (amide I band) and 1545 cm−1 (amide ΙΙ band) (Fig. 1, supplementary material). The absorption band at 1124 cm−1 corresponds to C-S. The stretching vibration of the S-S bond emerges at 525 cm−1, confirming papain's immobilization [48].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of X-ray diffraction spectroscopy for the nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para: The model of the crystalline structure of NGQDs, NGQD-Glu, and NGQD-Glu-Papain was analyzed using X-ray diffraction spectroscopy (XRD) (Fig. 1A). The XRD diagram of NGQDs shows a single diffraction peak around 2θ = 26° (interlayer space d = 3.5 Å), indicating the formation of the NGQDs structure with a high degree of crystalline morphology [49].

As shown in Fig. 1A, the XRD spectroscopy for NGQD-Glu showed a single peak around 2θ = 21° (interlayer space d=4.2 Å), and NGQD-Glu-Papain exhibited a single peak around 2θ = 20° (interlayer space d=4.45 Å), indicating the formation of NGQD-Glu and NGQD-Glu-Papain. The results showed that according to Bragg's law, with the binding of the glutaraldehyde linker, the interlayer spacing of the NGQDs sheets was significantly increased and reduced the crystallinity. Also, immobilization of papain has increased interlayer slightly [50].

The morphology and size of the synthesized NGQDs were investigated using transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and field emission scanning electron microscopes (SEM) measurements. The TEM and SEM (Fig. 1B and C) of NGQDs exhibited that the NGQDs has a spherical shape with an average diameter of 8–12 nm. The SEM result of NGQD-Glu-Papain showed that the morphology of the nanoparticles was a spherical form with an average diameter of about 45 nm (Fig. 1D). Consequently, as a result, after papain immobilization on the NGQDs, the size of the nanoparticles increased.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Energy diffusion spectral and thermogravimetric
ce:section.ce:para: The measurement of the energy-scattering X-ray spectrum (EDX) was used to conﬁrm the presence of elements in the structure of nanoparticles ( Fig. 2A, supplementary material). Carbon, nitrogen, oxygen, and sulfur were found on the surface of NGQD-Glu-Papain. The presence of sulfur on NGQD-Glu-Papain confirmed papain immobilization since the papain enzyme contains sulfur groups (related to cysteine residues).

Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used for thermal analysis. TGA charts showed the distinction in the structures of three compounds, namely NGQDs, NGQD-Glu, and NGQD-Glu-Papain. The TGA results of NGQDs (Fig. 2B, supplementary material) indicated decomposition in four steps. The water was removed at 70 °C with a total weight loss of 3.89%, and unstable functional groups containing oxygen and nitrogen were removed at 157 °C, with a weight loss of 8.92%. The next stage of decomposition was 278 °C, with a total weight loss of 22.17%, corresponding to a stable, functional group such as carboxyl, amine, and amide groups. The final step corresponding to the pyrolysis of the central core of NGQDs such as pyridine and pyrrolidine groups occurs at 464 °C, with a total weight loss of 23.64% [51]. The TGA results of NGQD-Glu (Fig. 2C, supplementary material) showed three decomposition steps. The first step in eliminating water occurs at 57.5 °C, with a weight loss of 4.62%. The next step was at 333 °C (with 30.28% weight loss), indicating that glutaraldehyde binds to functional groups of NGQDs. The peak at 157 °C was disappeared, which shows that the unstable functional groups on NGQDs have been successfully stabled. The final decomposition step occurred at 617 °C with a total weight loss of 23.8%, corresponding to the pyrolysis of the central core of NGQDs. Comparing the TGA results of NGQDs and NGQD-Glu shows that NGQD-Glu has become more stable than NGQDs due to the increased destruction temperature of the central core from 464 °C to 617 °C. The TGA results of NGQD-Glu-Papain (Fig. 2D, supplementary material) exhibited two decomposition steps. The first step was eliminating water which occurs at 58.5 °C, with a weight loss of 3.72%. The next step occurred at 390 °C (with a weight loss of 50.88%), corresponding to the elimination of stable, functional groups. The increase in the degradation temperature of stable, functional groups from 330 °C to 390 °C indicates that the enzyme was immobilized with covalent bonds on functional groups of NGQDs, which causes more stability of the groups, and increases the immobilized enzyme stability [52].

In this study, the immobilization yield was 78%, and the enzyme loading was 70 mg g−1. Xiangyu et al. showed that the yield of immobilization of papain on graphene oxide nanosheets is around 80% [53], which is similar to the immobilization yield of papain on NGQDs (in this work). In the other work, it has been shown that the loading amount of papain on magnetic gold nanocomposite was 54 mg g−1 [54]. Therefore, the loading amount of papain on NGQDs (in this work) was more than the enzyme loading on magnetic gold nanocomposite.

The results showed that the specific activity of the free and immobilized enzyme was 788 U mg−1 and 57 U mg−1, respectively, which shows that the relative activity of the immobilized enzyme to the free enzyme was 7.2% (the results not shown). The significant decrease in the relative activity of the enzyme can be correlated with the more stabilization of the enzyme (or increase in the enzyme rigidity) during the immobilization.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of optimal pH and temperature for activity and kinetic parameters
ce:section.ce:para: The changing of pH in the reaction medium can affect enzymes activity. The effect of pH on the enzyme activity was examined at different pHs from 3 to 10. As shown in Fig. 2A, the optimal pH for activity was 7.0 for both free and immobilized papain. It has been shown that the immobilization of papain on some support, such as cellulose beads, does not affect the optimal pH of enzyme activity [32]. Therefore, as shown in this work, NGQDs as support has no significant effect on the optimal pH for papain activity. The results (Fig. 2A) showed that in the acidic (pH 3–4.5) and alkaline (pH 8–10) medium, immobilized papain activity was higher than free papain, probably due to the acidic (COOH) and alkaline (NH2 and COO-) groups on the surface of NGQDs which decrease the effect of the pH on the immobilized enzyme activity.

One of the essential and influential parameters of enzymatic activity is temperature. To evaluate the effect of temperature on free and immobilized papain activity, the enzyme's activity was determined at different temperatures (25–90 °C). The results showed that immobilization of papain on NGQDs increased the optimum temperature for the activity of the enzyme from 70 °C to 75 °C (Fig. 2B). In the other work, it has been shown that immobilization of papain on chitosan-activated glutaraldehyde optimal temperature was increased to 80 °C [32]. Enzyme immobilization increases the rigidity of the enzyme structure, which leads to an increase in the enzyme's optimal temperature for activity [15,53]. The high optimal temperature for the activity of enzymes is an essential feature for proteases in industrial processes [55]. Therefore, NGQD-Glu-Papain is suitable for industrial applications relative to free papain.

Typically, the immobilization of the enzyme affects the kinetic parameters enzymatic properties (Vmax and Km) of enzymes. Then, the effect of immobilization on the kinetic properties of the free and immobilized papain was studied by measuring the enzyme's activity in various concentrations of casein (as a substrate) (Fig. 2C and D). The results demonstrate that the enzyme immobilization on NGQDs decreases the Michaelis constant (Km) of papain from 2.02 g L−1 (for free papain) to 1.20 g L−1 (for immobilized papain) (Fig. 2C and D, and Table 1). Compared to the free enzyme, the decrease in Km for immobilized papain indicates that the enzyme affinity to the substrate increased. Probably the enzyme immobilization is so performed that it causes the enzyme's active site to be accessible to the solution containing substrate. On the other hand, the ionic groups such as COO- and NH3 + on the surface of the support can interact with the substrate electrostatically, leading to the increase of local concentration of the substrate on NGQDs and then decrease in the apparent Km of the immobilized enzyme [56,57]. The results showed that papain immobilization significantly decreased the enzyme's Vmax from 932.1 μmol mg−1 min−1 to 62.5 μmol mg−1 min−1 (Fig. 2C, D, and Table 1). It has been shown that enzyme immobilization increases the rigidity of the immobilized enzyme [58]. The increase in the enzyme structure rigidity decreases the flexibility of the enzyme's active site and leads to decreases in enzyme activity [59,60]. Therefore, the significant reduction in immobilized papain activity can be correlated with the enzyme's rigidity (or stability), which is achieved by the enzyme immobilization on NGQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of immobilization on stability and reusability
ce:section.ce:para: Enzyme immobilization is one of the best ways to stabilize enzymes [61]. For this reason, the thermal stability of the free and immobilized enzymes was studied by incubating the enzyme at different temperatures (25–90 °C) for 30 min. The results indicated that as the incubation temperature increases, the stability of the free enzyme decreases ( Fig. 3A). Unlike the free enzyme, the immobilized enzyme activity (stability) was increased up to 85 °C, which demonstrating the significant increase in the stability of the immobilized enzyme [32].

The time-course thermal stability of the enzyme was determined at 70 °C during various times (0–150 min). As shown in Fig. 3B, the free enzyme's residual activity was 55% after incubation at 70 °C for 50 min, and the enzyme lost its activity (or stability) ultimately, after incubation for 60 min at 70 °C. The immobilized papain maintained 80% of its activity after incubation at 70 °C for 150 min. The results showed that the immobilization of papain on NGQDs significantly enhanced the stability of the enzyme. The physical and chemical properties of the support (NGQDs), such as high thermal stability and its ability to bind covalently and interact electrostatically with the enzyme, probably, are responsible for significant increases in the stability of the immobilized enzyme.

Further study of the effect of immobilization of papain on NGQDs on the stability of the enzyme, the storage stability of free and immobilized papain at 4 °C was determined in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). The results showed that the free papain completely loses its activity after only five days (Fig. 3C), while immobilized papain showed excellent storage stability than the free papain and maintained its activity (more than 20% of its activity) after three months (Fig. 3D). Gu et al. showed that immobilization of papain on graphene oxide causes a moderate increase in the enzyme stability [53]. However, in this work, it was observed that immobilization on NGQDs significantly increased enzyme stability. The NGQDs have different chemical and physical structures than graphene oxide [62]. The presence of nitrogen groups (such as amine and amide) in NGQDs causes more interactions (ionic and electrostatic) between the support and the enzyme and leads to more stabilization of the immobilized papain on NGQDs in comparison with graphene oxide. The high thermal stability of the NGQDs causes them to be proper support for the immobilization of enzymes. Indeed, the NGQDs limit the flexibility of enzyme molecules and hinder their denaturation in high-temperature incubation [15].

The enzymes are used as catalysts in organic synthesis [61]. A significant challenge in the using of enzymes as a catalyst in organic solvents is the low activity and stability of the enzymes [63]. Papain has been used as a catalyst in organic syntheses such as Knoevenagel and aldol reactions [63,64]. Therefore, the study of papain stability in the presence of organic solvents and increasing its stability by immobilization can be crucial in using the enzyme as a catalyst. To investigate the influence of organic solvents on the stability of the free and immobilized papain, the enzymes were incubated in 10% of each solvent (DMF, THF, ethanol, DMSO) at 70 °C at different times. The results showed that the free enzyme lost its activity after 60 min in the presence of solvents (DMF, THF, ethanol, DMSO). In the case of THF solvent, the free enzyme loses its activity after only 3 min (Fig. 3E). The stability of the free enzyme in the presence of the solvents was as follows: solvent-free> DMF > ethanol > DMSO > THF. The stability of the immobilized enzyme showed that the enzyme maintains more than 50% of its activity in the presence of different organic solvents after 200 min incubation at 70 °C (Fig. 3F). The stability of the immobilized enzyme in the presence of solvents was as follows: DMSO> ethanol > solvent-free > DMF > THF. The results showed that immobilization of papain on NGQDs significantly increased the enzyme stability in the presence of the solvents, particularly in the presence of polar solvents such as DMSO and ethanol. The polar organic solvent such as DMSO and ethanol disrupts the protein structure by H-bonding [65,66]. NGQDs have some groups, such as COOH (COO-) and OH, which can weaken the H-bonds between the polar solvents and the immobilized enzyme and lead to more stabilization of the immobilized enzyme in the polar solvents. Therefore, NGQD-Glu-Papain can be used as a catalyst in organic syntheses.

Reusability of the immobilized enzymes is the most crucial goal in using the enzymes in the chemical industries [10]. The reusability of the immobilized papain was studied in 50 mM of sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.5). The results showed that after five cycles, the immobilized papain maintains 65% of its activity (Fig. 3G). The reusability study shows that papain's immobilization is suitable for reusing the enzyme.

NGQDs were crystalline semiconductor nanomaterials with fluorometric properties due to quantum confinement effects [67]. In this study, the fluorometric properties of NGQDs and NGQD-Glu-Papain were examined. The results showed that NGQDs and NGQD-Glu-Papain have a fluorescence behavior dependent on excitation (shown in Fig. 3 and Fig. 4 in the supplementary material). The highest significant fluorescence peak of the NGQDs was at 433 nm when excited at 360 nm (Fig. 3 in the supplementary material), which is similar to the reported fluorometric properties of the NGQDs [68]. The maximum fluorescence intensity for NGQD-Glu-Papain was shown at 479 nm when the exciting wavelength was 400 nm (Fig. 4 in the supplementary material). The fluorescence intensity of NGQDs, NGQD-Glu, and NGQD-Glu-Papain was studied at the same concentration and the excitation wavelength at 420 nm. Electrostatic interactions [69], fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) [70,71], and the formation of a complex between GQDs with fluorescence quenching material [8] cause quenching of fluorescence in GQDs. In this work, the functionalization of NGQDs with glutaraldehyde decreases fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4A) due to reduce electron density in the core of NGQDs. In addition, the binding of papain to NGQD-Glu further reduces the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain (Fig. 4A). Physical interaction between the papain and the support leads to electron-poor density distribution, decreasing the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain [47,72].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study of the properties and specificity of the biosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Papain has been shown to have a strong affinity for cystatin C; therefore, the enzyme can be used to detect cystatin C [73]. To assess the properties of the NGQD-Glu-Papain biosensor, the effect of different concentrations of cystatin C (0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1) was studied on the fluorometric intensity NGQD-Glu-Papain. According to the results (Fig. 4B), the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain was increased with the addition of cystatin C. Nonspecific electrostatic interactions between papain and NGQDs cause quenching of fluorescence intensity [6,74]. Cystatin C can bind to papain (a cysteine protease inhibitor) with more favorable interactions [30]. Then, an increase in cystatin C concentration leads to an increase in the specific interaction between papain and cystatin C and a decrease in the nonspecific interaction between papain and NGQDs [72]. Therefore, the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain increases with an increase in cystatin C concentration. As shown in Fig. 4C, there is a linear relationship between (F-F0)/F0 and the logarithmic concentration of cystatin C. This linear relationship in the concentration range of 0.05 ng L−1 to 50 ng L−1 makes the NGQD-Glu-papain a biosensor to detect cystatin C. the results showed that the lowest concentration of cystatin C detected by NGQD-Glu-Papain biosensor was 0.05 ng L−1. In the recent work, Desai et al. showed that the screen-printed multi-walled electrode (SPMWE) sensor papain-base has a limit detection of 0.58 ng L−1 ( Table 2) [30]. Therefore, the fluorometric sensor made in this work is more sensitive than the SPMWE sensor and previous sensors for detecting the low concentration of cystatin C.

To evaluate the specificity of the biosensor, the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain was investigated in the presence of two concentrations (0.05 ng L−1 and 50 ng L−1) of different proteins. The result showed that bovine serum albumin (BSA), papain, and human serum albumin (HSA) decreased the fluorescence intensity of NGQD-Glu-Papain, whereas cystatin C increased the fluorescence intensity (Fig. 4D). Therefore, one can conclude that NGQD-Glu-Papain is a specific biosensor for cystatin C.

This work uses NGQDs as support for papain immobilization. The results showed that the chemical and physical interaction between the papain and the NGQDs causes immobilized papain's high temperature and storage stability. The stability results organic solvents showed that papain immobilization on NGQDs significantly increased the enzyme stability in the presence of polar solvents (DMSO and ethanol). Due to significant improvement in enzyme stability, NGQDs is proper support for enzyme stabilization. The results showed that the significant decrease in the relative activity and Vmax of the immobilized enzyme could be correlated with more rigidity (or stability) of the immobilized papain. In this work, we synthesize a highly sensitive protein-protein interaction fluorescence biosensor for the detection of cystatin C. The NGQD-Glu-Papain sensor was an ultra-sensitive and fast sensor that could detect cystatin C at a minimum detection limit (0.05 ng L−1) over time (5 min). The storage stability results showed that NGQD-Glu-Papain remains stable for three months (in the refrigerator).
